success_surface: Filtering and transferring data between worksheets with precise positional control
effective_behaviors: Separate data processing (pandas) from structured output writing (openpyxl)
Use explicit row/column indexing when target positions are specified in instructions
Create new workbook/sheet objects when instructed to place results in new locations
Copy headers from source dataframe to preserve schema alignment
Properly manage workbook lifecycle by closing loaded files after use
Construct boolean filter conditions programmatically for multi-condition queries
Verify output structure matches instruction-specified offsets before saving
evidence: Solution loaded data via pandas.read_excel() for filtering efficiency, then wrote via openpyxl.cell() for positional precision
Explicitly set headers at row=2 and data at row=3 per instruction requirements
Created fresh Workbook instance rather than modifying existing sheet structure
Iterated through df.columns.tolist() to copy headers maintaining column order
Closed src_wb with src_wb.close() to release file handles properly
Used iloc with column indices (1, 2) corresponding to columns B and C for filtering conditions
Code executed successfully without data corruption or format loss

memory_items.title: Pandas-Openpyxl Hybrid Workflow
memory_items.lesson: Use pandas for data logic/processing and openpyxl for structured output placement
memory_items.generalization: When tasks require both data transformation and precise cell positioning, leverage pandas for efficient DataFrame operations and openpyxl for writing specific cell locations with exact row/column offsets.

memory_items.title: Explicit Positional Targeting
memory_items.lesson: Always translate instruction-specified positions into explicit row/column parameters
memory_items.generalization: When instructions specify where to place content (e.g., headers at row N, data at row M+1), map these directly to function arguments rather than relying on default append behavior.

memory_items.title: Header Schema Preservation
memory_items.lesson: Extract and reapply column headers to maintain source-to-target schema mapping
memory_items.generalization: When copying data to new sheets/workbooks, retrieve headers from the source DataFrame and write them explicitly to preserve column correspondence.

memory_items.title: Workbook Resource Management
memory_items.lesson: Close all opened workbook objects to prevent file lock issues
memory_items.generalization: Any workbook loaded with load_workbook() or created with Workbook() should have its handle closed after operations complete, especially before subsequent file operations.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Hybrid Pandas-Openpyxl Pattern
When tasks combine data filtering/transformation with precise output placement:
- Use pandas.read_excel() + DataFrame operations for complex filtering logic (multiple conditions, calculations)
- Switch to openpyxl.Workbook.write() for controlling exact row/column positions in output
- Do not rely on pandas.to_excel() when specific cell offsets (e.g., headers at row 2, data at row 3) are required

### Positional Specification Protocol
When instructions specify where to place content:
1. Parse the row/column coordinates explicitly from instructions
2. Map headers to their designated row parameter
3. Offset data rows accordingly (typically headers_row + 1)
4. Write iteratively if complex formatting or validation is needed

### Header Migration Practice
When transferring data to new sheets/workbooks:
- Extract column names from source DataFrame (.columns.tolist())
- Write headers before data rows to establish schema alignment
- Maintain original column order unless instructed otherwise

### Lifecycle Management
Always close workbook handles:
- Call .close() on any workbook loaded via load_workbook()
- Close before renaming/moving files to prevent Windows file locks
- Use context managers when possible for automatic cleanup
